english: Cs.: itch, za-rkóṅ (za rkong) id.; Lt.: rkóṅ-po (rkong po). Others describe it as a scabby eruption of the skin, chiefly affect-ing animals, but occasionally also men C.

also skón-pa (skon pa) 1. basket; the word is said to be used in Kun.; perh. also the Ladakian word *kun-dúm* (*kun dum*), a large cylindrical or bottle-shaped basket, may be traced to the same form. I never found it in books. - 2. net, fowler's net Lex.

also skyag(-pa) (skyag (-pa)), dirt, excrement; *kyág-pa tóṅ-wa* (*kyag pa tong wa*) C., *kyág táṅ-če* (*kyag tang ce*) W. to cack, vulg. - mig-skyág (mig skyag) the impurity in the eyes Cs.; *na-skyág* (*na skyag*) ear-wax W.

the wild ass or horse of Cen-tral Asia, Chigitai, p'o-rkyaṅ (pho rkyang) male, mo-rkyaṅ (mo rkyang) female of it; rkyaṅ dár-ma (rkyang dar ma) a young wild ass, rkyaṅ-rgan (rkyang rgan) an old one, Cs. - rkyaṅ-ču (rkyang chu) n. of a lake in the south of Ld., in the neighbourhood of which these animals are particularly numerous.

simple, single; ras rkyaṅ (ras rkyang) a single sheet of cotton cloth Dzl., Mil.; *mi kyaṅ* (*mi kyang*) a single i. e. free, un-employed man, one that carries no burden C.; yi-ge rkyaṅ-pa (yi ge rkyang pa) a letter that forms by itself a syllable, or one that is not brtségs-pa (brtsegs pa) and without any other consonant or a vowel-sign superscribed; rkyaṅ-pai graṅs (rkyang pa'i grangs) are called 1, 10, 100 and the further powers of 10; miṅ rkyaṅ-pa (ming rkyang pa) a word that has no affix-denoting case etc. attached. - *kyaṅ* (*kyang*), *kyaṅ-kyaṅ* (*kyang kyang*), *kyáṅ-ka* (*kyang ka*), *ka-(r)kyáṅ* (*ka (r)kyang*), col. (in B. śa-stag (sha stag)) only, nothing but, *pé-ča ṅags kár-kyaṅ dug* (*pe cha ngags kar kyang dug*) the book contains nothing but charms. - *kyaṅ-kyáṅ* (*kyang kyang*) also: living by one's self, childless W. - *kyaṅ-ltab* (*kyang ltab*) single folded.
n. of a vein, v. rtsa (rtsa).
(Ld. *skyan* (*skyan*)) 1. a brass-vessel like a tea-pot, with a spout, rag-rkyan (rag rkyan) id.; *o-kyan* (*o kyan*) W. milk-pot. - 2. pot-belly, paunch Sch.

leather bag frq.; p'uṅ-po mi-gtsaṅ-rdzás-kyi rkyál-pa (phung po mi gtsang rdzas kyi rkyal pa) a poetical term for the body Mil.; rkyal-bu (rkyal bu) (*kyal-lu* (*kyal lu*)) small bag, pouch; ra-rkyál (ra rkyal) bag of goatskin; p'ye-rkyál (phye rkyal) (*če-kyal* (*che kyal*) C., *pe-ky.* (*pe ky.*) W.) bag for flour.
to swim, *kyal gyáb-če* (*kyal gyab ce*) W. id.; rkyal rtséd-pa (rkyal rtsed pa) to amuse one's self by swimming.

Will.: 'with Buddhists: a co-operating cause, the concurrent occasion of an event as distinguished from its proximate (or rather primary, original) cause', rgyu (rgyu) हेतु. (The right meaning was given already by Schr., whereas after-wards, by a mistake of Cs., the totally erroneous sense of 'effect, consequence' has become current among philologists.) 1. cause, occasion, rkyén-gis (rkyen gis) c. gen. by, on account of, čii rkyén-gis (ci'i rkyen gis) whereby? dei rkyén-gyis (de'i rkyen gyis) thereby, therefore, dei rgyu dei rkyén-gyis (de'i rgyu de'i rkyen gyis) id. As a medical term, opp. to rgyu (rgyu) (the anthropological or primary cause of a disease) it denotes the patho-logical or secondary cause of it. - 2. event, occurrence, accident, case, circum-stance, in a general sense, in as far as the Buddhist conceives every thing that happens in the mutual connexion of cause and effect; rkyen ṅán-pa (rkyen ngan pa) unfortunate ac-cident; rkyen ṅán-pas °das (rkyen ngan pas 'das) he has perished by a fatal accident Glr.; tse °dir byúṅ-bai rkyen ṅán-rnams (tshe 'dir byung ba'i rkyen ngan rnams) the adversities of the present life Mil.; raṅ mi °dód-pai rkyen (rang mi 'dod pa'i rkyen) an event disagreeable to one's own self; bló-bur rkyen (blo bur rkyen) a sudden accident Mil.; rkyen dé-la brtén-nas (rkyen de la brten nas) owing to that cir-cumstance Tar. 8. 1. méd-pai rkyén-la bltás-te (med pa'i rkyen la bltas te) or brtén-te (brten te) C. considering the case of not being..., not having..., thus nad-kyi rkyen (nad kyi rkyen), čí-bai rkyen ('chi ba'i rkyen) stands also for: a case of disease, of death; gal-
